title: Interfaces
subtitle: Write a promise once, and let many different classes keep it their own way.
goal: Learn what an interface is, write your own with interface and implements, let one class implement several interfaces at once, use default methods, and understand when to pick an interface over an abstract class.

blocks.heading: An interface is a promise 🤝
blocks.html:
Imagine a wall socket. Anything with the right plug can use it — a lamp, a phone charger, a toaster. The socket doesn't care what you plug in, only that you have the right plug. That plug shape is a contract: "if you fit, you work."

In Java an interface is exactly that kind of contract. It lists method names a class promises to provide, but it doesn't say how. Each class that signs the contract fills in the details its own way.

blocks.heading: Writing one: interface and implements
blocks.html:
You create a contract with the word interface. Inside, you list methods with no body — just the promise:

interface Drawable { void draw(); }

A class signs the contract with the word implements. When it does, it must write a real draw() method, or Java refuses to run:

class Circle implements Drawable { ... }

blocks.caption: Two classes keep the same promise differently
blocks.output:
Drawing a round circle: O
Drawing a square: []
blocks.explain: Drawable is the contract — it just promises a draw() method. Circle and Square each keep that promise in their own way. Notice we can call draw() on both because both signed the same contract.

blocks.html: Methods in an interface have no body. You write void draw(); with a semicolon, not { } curly braces. The body lives in the class that implements it.

blocks.heading: One class, many contracts 🎯
blocks.html:
Remember inheritance? A class can only extends one parent — that's single inheritance. But a class can implements as many interfaces as you like, separated by commas!

A duck can be both a Swimmer and a Runner. It just signs both contracts: class Duck implements Swimmer, Runner.

blocks.heading: Default methods (Java 8+)
blocks.html:
Normally interface methods have no body. But sometimes you want to share a ready-made method that every class gets for free. That's a default method — it uses the word default and does have a body:

default void describe() { ... }

Classes get it automatically and can use it as-is, or replace it with their own version.

blocks.caption: A Duck implements TWO interfaces, plus a free default method
blocks.output:
The duck paddles across the pond.
The duck waddles along the shore.
I can move on land and in water!
blocks.explain: Duck signs both Swimmer and Runner, so it must write swim() and run(). It gets describe() for free because Runner gave it a default body — Duck never wrote describe() itself!

blocks.heading: Interface vs abstract class — which one? 🤔
blocks.html:
Both let you list methods that subclasses must fill in, so when do you pick which?

  • Interface — use it for a can-do ability that unrelated things might share. A Bird and a Plane have nothing in common, yet both are Flyable. A class can implement many interfaces.
  • Abstract class — use it for an is-a family that shares real code and fields. A Dog and a Cat are both an Animal and share a name field. A class can extend only one.

Rule of thumb: shared ability → interface; shared family with shared code → abstract class.

blocks.heading: Programming to an interface
blocks.html:
Here's the superpower. If your code only mentions the interface (the contract) instead of any one class, it works for every class that signs that contract — even ones you write later!

A method that takes a Flyable can fly a Bird today and a Plane tomorrow, with zero changes. This is called programming to an interface, and it keeps code flexible.

blocks.caption: One method, any Flyable thing
blocks.output:
The bird flaps its wings and soars.
The plane roars down the runway and lifts off.
blocks.explain: letItFly() only knows about Flyable, not Bird or Plane. So it happily flies BOTH. If you invented a Rocket that implements Flyable, letItFly would work on it too — no changes needed. That's programming to an interface.

blocks.html: Java already gives you interfaces! Comparable is a built-in contract with one method, compareTo. Any class that implements it can be sorted automatically. You're already surrounded by interfaces.

blocks.question: What does an interface actually contain?
options: A list of methods a class promises to provide (the contract)
The full working code for every method
A copy of the main() method
blocks.explain: An interface is a contract: it lists method names a class promises to provide. The real code lives in the class that implements it (except for default methods).

blocks.question: How many interfaces can a single class implement?
options: As many as you want, separated by commas
blocks.explain: Unlike extends (single inheritance, one parent only), a class can implement many interfaces: class Duck implements Swimmer, Runner.

blocks.question: You have unrelated things — a Bird and a Plane — that should both be able to fly. What fits best?
options: An abstract class they both extend
A Flyable interface they both implement
Copy the fly() code into each one separately
blocks.explain: A shared can-do ability across unrelated classes is exactly what an interface is for. Bird and Plane aren't the same family, so an abstract class is a poor fit.

blocks.title: Sign your first contract
blocks.instructions: Finish the Robot class so it keeps the Greetable promise. Write a greet() method that prints a greeting from the robot. Your code must use implements and the interface Greetable.
blocks.passMsg: Contract signed! Robot kept its promise to greet.
blocks.failMsg: Keep the Greetable interface and Robot implements Greetable, and make greet() print a greeting.

blocks.title: One animal, two abilities
blocks.instructions: Make the Frog implement BOTH Swimmer and Jumper. Fill in swim() to print how the frog swims, and jump() to print how it jumps. Your code must implement both interfaces.
blocks.solutionOutput:
The frog kicks through the water.
The frog leaps high into the air!
blocks.passMsg: Two contracts, one frog — that's the power interfaces give you!
blocks.failMsg: Frog must implement Swimmer, Jumper (both, comma-separated) and fill in swim() and jump().

blocks.title: Surprise Quiz! Days 11–15
blocks.intro: Pop quiz! Let's see what stuck from the last 5 days. No grades, just fun 🎉

questions.question: What do you use to create a new object from a class?
questions.explain: new builds a fresh object from a class, for example: Dog d = new Dog(); (Day 11!)

questions.question: Why do we make fields private and use getters and setters?
options: To make the code longer for fun
To hide and protect the data so it can only change in safe ways
questions.explain: That's encapsulation: keeping fields private protects them, and setters can check that new values are valid. (Day 12!)

questions.question: Which keyword lets one class reuse the code of another (a parent class)?
questions.explain: A subclass uses extends to inherit fields and methods from its parent (superclass). (Day 13!)

questions.question: What does @Override tell Java?
options: This method replaces a method from the parent class
questions.explain: @Override marks a method that re-defines (overrides) one from the parent, and lets Java double-check you did it right. (Day 14!)

questions.question: Which keyword lets a class promise to provide certain methods, using an interface?
questions.explain: A class uses implements to fulfill an interface, promising to provide all the methods the interface lists. (Day 15!)

blocks.scenario: Scenario: You're building a student grade system. Different grading strategies (percentage-based, pass/fail) should all work through the same interface. Use an interface to define a contract, then two classes implement it differently.
blocks.caption: Flexible grading system using interfaces
blocks.output:
Score 85:
  Percent grade: B
  Pass/Fail:     PASS
Score 45:
  Percent grade: F
  Pass/Fail:     FAIL
blocks.explain: Both PercentGrader and PassFailGrader implement the Grader interface but grade differently. The main method uses the interface type, so it works with any grading strategy — that's programming to an interface (Day 15), using classes (Day 11), encapsulation (Day 12), and conditionals (Day 6).

blocks.title: Milestone Exam — Days 11–15
blocks.intro: OOP checkpoint! Classes, objects, encapsulation, inheritance, and interfaces. 70% to pass.

questions.question: Which keyword creates a new object from a class?
questions.explain: The new keyword builds a fresh object from a class blueprint, e.g. Dog d = new Dog(); (Day 11)

questions.question: Why do we make fields private and use getters/setters?
options: To hide and protect data so it changes only in safe ways
questions.explain: Encapsulation hides internal data. Setters can validate new values before accepting them. (Day 12)

questions.question: Which keyword lets a subclass reuse code from a parent class?
questions.explain: A subclass uses extends to inherit fields and methods from its superclass. (Day 13)

questions.question: What does @Override do?
options: Marks that this method replaces one from the parent
Prevents the method from being called
questions.explain: @Override tells Java this method intentionally replaces one from the parent class, and Java checks the signature matches. (Day 14)

questions.question: How many interfaces can a single class implement?
questions.explain: A class can implement any number of interfaces, separated by commas: class Duck implements Swimmer, Runner. (Day 15)

blocks.scenario: Scenario: You're building a student grade system. Different grading strategies (percentage-based, pass/fail) should all work through the same interface.
blocks.caption: Flexible grading system using interfaces
blocks.output:
Score 85:
  Percent grade: B
  Pass/Fail:     PASS
Score 45:
  Percent grade: F
  Pass/Fail:     FAIL
blocks.explain: Both graders implement the Grader interface but grade differently. The main method uses the interface type — that's programming to an interface (Day 15).

blocks.title: Milestone Exam — Days 11–15
blocks.intro: OOP checkpoint! Classes, encapsulation, inheritance, and interfaces. 70% to pass.

questions.question: Which keyword creates a new object from a class?
questions.explain: The new keyword builds a fresh object from a class blueprint. (Day 11)

questions.question: Why make fields private and use getters/setters?
options: To hide and protect data so it changes only in safe ways
questions.explain: Encapsulation hides internal data. Setters can validate values. (Day 12)

questions.question: Which keyword lets a subclass reuse code from a parent?
questions.explain: A subclass uses extends to inherit fields and methods. (Day 13)

questions.question: What does @Override do?
options: Marks that this method replaces one from the parent
Prevents the method from being called
questions.explain: @Override tells Java this method replaces one from the parent class. (Day 14)

questions.question: How many interfaces can a single class implement?
questions.explain: A class can implement any number of interfaces, separated by commas. (Day 15)

challenge.title: Build a Pet contract with a default method
challenge.instructions: Create an interface Pet with a method makeSound() and a DEFAULT method play() that prints a generic play message. Make a Cat that implements Pet and prints a cat sound (it should use the free default play()). Make a Dog that implements Pet, prints a dog sound, AND writes its OWN play() that prints something dog-specific. In main, create both as Pet, then call makeSound() and play() on each.
challenge.solutionOutput:
Meow!
This pet loves to play!
Woof!
The dog fetches the ball!
challenge.hint: Cat doesn't write play() at all — it gets the default one for free, so it prints the generic message. Dog writes its own play(), which replaces (overrides) the default. That's why their play() outputs differ.
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 Day 15 complete! You wrote a contract, implemented it two ways, and used a default method. Interfaces are how big Java programs stay flexible — you're thinking like a real engineer now.
